# Tests import config.mjs (operator-overlay) instead of config.template.mjs (canonical defaults) — drift risk across ~78 imports

## Context

Operator-flagged architectural concern surfaced 2026-05-25 during the session that landed [#11969](https://github.com/neomjs/neo/pull/11969) (the corrective config-overlay-import fix):

> i have one more item: inside our ai folder, we want to use /config.mjs . however, i found 86 occurrences for config files inside our test folder. this is dangerous: gitignored files, users can change values, which could then break our tests. challenge: would it be smarter to use config.template files for our tests?

## The Problem

After #11969, runtime code correctly imports from the operator-overlay `ai/config.mjs` (gitignored, auto-created from template via `initServerConfigs.mjs`). However, **the test suite still imports the same operator-overlay files**. This is a determinism / CI-vs-local-parity hazard:

- CI: fresh `npm install` runs `prepare`, which copies templates to `config.mjs` files → tests see template-default values.
- Local: operator customizes `ai/config.mjs` (e.g., sets `modelProvider: 'ollama'`) → tests assert on `'gemini'` default, fail locally, pass in CI.

Audit results from `grep -rn "config\.mjs" test/ --include="*.mjs"`:

- **78 import-style references** across the test suite (the broader 118-line count includes path-strings in comments/strings).

| Target | Import Count | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `ai/mcp/server/memory-core/config.mjs` | 46 | Highest leverage |
| `ai/mcp/server/knowledge-base/config.mjs` | 18 | |
| `ai/mcp/server/github-workflow/config.mjs` | 8 | |
| `ai/mcp/server/neural-link/config.mjs` | 4 | |
| `ai/config.mjs` (top-level) | 3 | Post-#11969 additions only |
| `/tmp/*-config.mjs` | 13 | Test-fixture temp files — **NOT real drift**, isolated by design |

The actual drift surface is **~65 imports** (excluding the `/tmp` fixtures).

## The Architectural Reality

Three usage patterns across the 65 imports:

1. **Mutation-style** (`aiConfig.storagePaths.graph = testDbPath`) — writes to the reactive `Neo.ai.Config` singleton. Template vs mjs both target the same singleton (per the idempotent `Neo.setupClass` from #11969). **No drift risk.**
2. **Assertion-style** (`expect(config.modelProvider).toBe('gemini')`) — reads default values for comparison. **This is the drift-vulnerable surface.**
3. **Setup-wiring** (`aiConfig.autoIngestFileSystem = false` to pin a known state before a test) — hybrid pattern; immune to overlay because it explicitly sets the value.

**Second-order concern from #11969**: per-server templates currently import `AiConfig from '../../../config.mjs'` (the top-level overlay). So even if a test imports the per-server **template**, it transitively reads operator-overlay values for Tier-1 fields. A pure suffix-swap doesn't close the leak.

## The Fix — Option C (test-fixture file)

**Recommended architecture**: a dedicated test-fixture layer that decouples the assert/mutation concerns:

```js
// test/playwright/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.mjs (NEW)

// Frozen snapshot of Tier-1 defaults. Tests that need to ASSERT on default
// values should compare against this, not against the live `aiConfig` instance
// (which may carry operator overlays from `ai/config.mjs`).
import AiConfig from '../../../ai/config.template.mjs';

export const TIER1_DEFAULTS = Object.freeze({...AiConfig.data});

// Re-export for tests that explicitly want the live singleton (mutation case).
// Same Neo.ai.Config instance any runtime code sees, courtesy of the idempotent
// setupClass registration from PR #11969.
export {AiConfig};
```

Per-server analog: `test/playwright/fixtures/memoryCoreDefaults.mjs`, `knowledgeBaseDefaults.mjs`, etc., each re-exporting from their respective `config.template.mjs`.

Test migration pattern:

```js
// BEFORE (drift-vulnerable):
import aiConfig from '../../../../../../ai/mcp/server/memory-core/config.mjs';
expect(aiConfig.modelProvider).toBe('gemini');

// AFTER (deterministic):
import {MEMORY_CORE_DEFAULTS} from '../../../../../../test/playwright/fixtures/memoryCoreDefaults.mjs';
expect(MEMORY_CORE_DEFAULTS.modelProvider).toBe('gemini');
```

Mutation-style imports stay as-is (no drift risk).

## Why Option C over the alternatives

Other options considered and rejected:

- **Option A — bulk-swap mjs → template in test imports**: doesn't close the second-order leak (per-server templates import top-level mjs). Half-fix.
- **Option B — rewire per-server templates to import top-level template**: breaks the runtime overlay chain (#11969 fix would regress). Anti-pattern.
- **Option D — pin overrides in beforeAll of each test**: requires per-test mutation knowledge; doesn't scale; brittle.

Option C separates the concerns cleanly: runtime untouched, tests get a deterministic fixture, no overlay chain leak.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `test/playwright/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.mjs` exists and exports `TIER1_DEFAULTS` (frozen snapshot of `ai/config.template.mjs`) and re-exports `AiConfig` (live singleton).
- [ ] Per-server fixture files exist for each server-group with `config.mjs` imports in tests: `memoryCoreDefaults.mjs`, `knowledgeBaseDefaults.mjs`, `githubWorkflowDefaults.mjs`, `neuralLinkDefaults.mjs`.
- [ ] All assertion-style `config.mjs` imports in tests are migrated to the fixture-snapshot pattern.
- [ ] Mutation-style imports are NOT migrated (target same singleton; no drift risk).
- [ ] An operator-side smoke verifies: with a customized `ai/config.mjs` (e.g., `modelProvider: 'openAiCompatible'`), tests still pass.
- [ ] No regression in the 200+ existing affected specs.

## Out of Scope

- Refactoring the operator-overlay mechanism itself.
- Touching the `/tmp/*-config.mjs` temporary fixtures (those are isolated test artifacts).
- Migrating the per-server templates' `AiConfig` import chain (anti-pattern per Option B rejection).

## Sub-Decomposition

This is an umbrella; subdivide by server-group for review-ability:

- [ ] Sub-1: top-level `ai/config.mjs` (3 imports — smallest, validates the fixture pattern)
- [ ] Sub-2: `knowledge-base` (18 imports)
- [ ] Sub-3: `github-workflow` (8 imports)
- [ ] Sub-4: `neural-link` (4 imports)
- [ ] Sub-5: `memory-core` (46 imports — largest; ship last after pattern is settled)

Per-server subs reference this umbrella.

## Avoided Traps

- ✓ Don't bulk-swap suffix only — doesn't close the second-order leak from per-server-template overlay chain.
- ✓ Don't rewire per-server templates back to top-level template — regresses #11969.
- ✓ Don't migrate mutation-style imports — they target the same Neo class singleton either way; no drift risk; churn without benefit.
- ✓ Don't add a "test-only" branch in the runtime config files — runtime-conditional behavior is an anti-pattern.

## Related

- #11969 — corrective PR that exposed this concern (runtime now imports overlay; tests still do too)
- #11912 (potentially related — broader test-substrate cleanup epic)
- `feedback_template_vs_overlay_import_v_b_a` (memory authority for the V-B-A discipline that catches this class of bug)

Handoff Retrieval Hint: "test-config drift overlay vs template Option C fixture file 65 imports umbrella".

- 2026-05-25T14:32:44Z @tobiu referenced in commit `e75a9a7` - "test(ai): add Tier-1 test fixture (TIER1_DEFAULTS) + migrate top-level config.mjs imports (#11977) (#11978)

* test(ai): add Tier-1 test fixture (TIER1_DEFAULTS) + migrate top-level config.mjs imports (#11977)

Sub-1 of #11976 — validates the fixture-file pattern (Option C from the umbrella)
before the larger per-server subs land.

## Scope

Two assertion-style imports of top-level `ai/config.mjs` in tests (both
introduced by #11969 in the same session as #11976 was filed):

- `test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/memory-core/config.template.spec.mjs`
- `test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/knowledge-base/config.template.spec.mjs`

These were drift-vulnerable: operator-customized `ai/config.mjs` (gitignored)
would silently break test assertions that compare per-server template fields
to Tier-1 defaults.

## The Fix

1. New fixture `test/playwright/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.mjs`:
   - Imports `AiConfig` from the tracked `ai/config.template.mjs` (never the
     gitignored overlay)
   - Exports `TIER1_DEFAULTS` = `Object.freeze({...AiConfig.data})` — frozen
     snapshot for deterministic assertions
   - Re-exports `AiConfig` for callers that need the live singleton (mutation
     pattern; same instance any runtime sees, courtesy of the idempotent
     setupClass registration from #11969)
   - JSDoc explains the determinism contract + second-order overlay-chain
     concern + which use case wants which export

2. Migrated both spec files:
   - `import AiConfig from '.../ai/config.mjs'` → `import {TIER1_DEFAULTS}
     from '.../test/playwright/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.mjs'`
   - Replaced `AiConfig.X` references with `TIER1_DEFAULTS.X` in assertion
     blocks (no behavioral change — same template-defined defaults)

## Test Evidence

- `npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/ai/config.template.spec.mjs
  test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/memory-core/config.template.spec.mjs
  test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/knowledge-base/config.template.spec.mjs
  test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/orchestrator/services/TenantRepoSyncService.spec.mjs`
  → 38/38 PASS (covers the parallel-worker collision scenario from #11969).

## Pattern validated for the remaining subs

This PR establishes the canonical migration pattern: per-server fixture files
re-exporting from their respective `config.template.mjs`, frozen snapshot for
assertions, live singleton re-exported for mutations. Subs 2–5 follow the
same shape against `knowledge-base`, `github-workflow`, `neural-link`, and
`memory-core` (largest, ships last).

* test(ai): deep-clone + deep-freeze TIER1_DEFAULTS + add fixture-contract spec (#11977 cycle-2)

Resolves @neo-gpt cycle-1 review on #11978 — TIER1_DEFAULTS shallow-freeze leak.

The cycle-1 implementation used `Object.freeze({...AiConfig.data})`, which:

  - Top-level-froze the spread result.
  - Preserved REFERENCES to nested groups (`auth`, `ollama`, `openAiCompatible`,
    `engines.chroma`, `orchestrator.intervals`, ...).
  - Made the nested groups identical references to the live `AiConfig.data` —
    `TIER1_DEFAULTS.auth === AiConfig.data.auth`, `Object.isFrozen(TIER1_DEFAULTS.auth)`
    was `false`, and mutating `AiConfig.data.auth.realm` silently propagated
    into `TIER1_DEFAULTS.auth.realm`.

The JSDoc claimed "mutation cannot leak into sibling specs through the
shared module cache". The implementation didn't satisfy that contract.

## Fix

1. `deepClone(value)`: custom recursive cloner that:
   - Passes primitives + functions through (functions stable for our snapshot purpose;
     `structuredClone` rejects the nested `dummyEmbeddingFunction` arrow funcs).
   - Recursively copies plain objects + arrays into fresh containers.
   - Severs every nested reference between snapshot and live singleton.

2. `deepFreeze(value)`: recursive Object.freeze across the cloned tree.

3. `TIER1_DEFAULTS = deepFreeze(deepClone(AiConfig.data))`: produces a truly
   immutable nested snapshot with independent references.

4. JSDoc updated to describe the actual contract (deep-clone severing
   references + recursive freeze), with explicit callout that the cycle-1
   shallow-freeze pattern was the failure mode.

## Regression-anchor spec

New `test/playwright/unit/test/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.spec.mjs` asserts
the fixture's own contract:

  1. Top-level snapshot is frozen.
  2. Nested groups (`auth`, `ollama`, `openAiCompatible`, `engines.chroma`,
     `orchestrator.intervals`) are individually frozen — would have failed
     under cycle-1's shallow freeze.
  3. Nested groups are independent references from `AiConfig.data` — would
     have failed under cycle-1's shared-reference path.
  4. Mutating `AiConfig.data.auth.realm` does NOT propagate to
     `TIER1_DEFAULTS.auth.realm` — direct regression-anchor for the leak
     GPT empirically demonstrated. Finally clause restores the live value
     so sibling specs don't observe drift.
  5. Sanity check that values match `ai/config.template.mjs` defaults (not
     overlay drift).

## Test evidence

  - `npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/test/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.spec.mjs` → 5/5 PASS
  - Full impact suite: fixture-contract + Tier-1 template + MC + KB + TenantRepoSync → 43/43 PASS

Pattern is now safe to scale to Subs 2–5; per-server fixtures can reuse
the same `deepClone` + `deepFreeze` helper shape.

* docs(ai): align fixture module JSDoc with shipped deepClone implementation (#11977 cycle-3)

Resolves @neo-gpt cycle-2 metadata-drift blocker.

The cycle-2 implementation replaced `structuredClone` with a custom recursive
`deepClone()` (because `structuredClone` rejects the nested arrow functions
in `aiConfig.dummyEmbeddingFunction`). The shipped helper JSDoc explained
the trade-off, but the module-level "Determinism contract" still claimed
"Deep-cloned via `structuredClone`" — a wording mismatch that would poison
future KB answers about the fixture contract.

Now matches the shipped code:
  - Names the custom `deepClone()` and `deepFreeze()` helpers explicitly
  - Calls out the `structuredClone`-rejection-on-arrow-funcs rationale
  - References `Object.freeze({...x})` as the cycle-1 failure mode, not the
    shipped pattern

No code change. 5/5 fixture-contract specs still pass.

* refactor(ai): use Neo.clone() instead of reinventing deepClone in fixture (#11977 cycle-4)

Operator-flagged duplication: cycle-2's custom recursive `deepClone()` is a
reinvention of Neo's canonical `Neo.clone(obj, true, true)`. See
`src/Neo.mjs:8-35,230-232`:

  - `cloneMap.Object()` iterates `Reflect.ownKeys()` and recurses via
    `Neo.clone(value, deep, ignoreNeoInstances)` — identical shape to my
    custom helper.
  - `cloneMap.Array()` does `obj.map(val => Neo.clone(val, deep, ...))`.
  - Functions + primitives flow through the `?.()` fallback unchanged.
  - `ignoreNeoInstances=true` matches the snapshot semantic (we don't want
    to clone Neo singleton instances, just data).

Drops the local `deepClone()` helper; imports `Neo` from `src/Neo.mjs` and
uses `Neo.clone(AiConfig.data, true, true)`.

Kept the local `deepFreeze()` — Neo doesn't ship a recursive-freeze
counterpart. JSDoc notes that if a `Neo.freeze` primitive lands later, this
helper retires.

Per `core.Base conventions rationale` memory: don't reinvent Neo primitives
without auditing GC pressure + ripple scope + historical constraint. The
shipped pattern matches Neo's existing convention exactly.

## Test evidence

  - `npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/test/fixtures/aiConfigDefaults.spec.mjs
    test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/memory-core/config.template.spec.mjs
    test/playwright/unit/ai/mcp/server/knowledge-base/config.template.spec.mjs`
    → 12/12 PASS. Snapshot contract preserved across the swap.

- 2026-05-25T23:39:28Z @tobiu referenced in commit `2949ef9` - "feat(test-fixtures): add KB_DEFAULTS + migrate SourcePathsConfig drift-vulnerable assertion (#11981) (#12001)

Sub-2 of #11976 — per-server fixture establishing the Sub-1 (#11977/#11978) pattern
at the per-server (KB) tier. Inlines KB-template canonical default values into a deep-
frozen snapshot for deterministic test assertions, mirroring `aiConfigDefaults.mjs`.

Per the ticket's V-B-A scope correction: only 1 of 18 KB specs is actually drift-
vulnerable (LearningSource base-directory test at SourcePathsConfig.spec.mjs:310,
which reads the live overlay-merged `aiConfig.sourcePaths?.LearningSource` then
asserts on the canonical 'learn' base). The other 17 use mutation/round-trip/setup-
wiring patterns that are drift-immune by design and intentionally left untouched.

KB_DEFAULTS covers KB-template-specific fields (sourcePaths, collectionName, path,
dataPath, hierarchyPath, etc.). Tier-1 fields (auth, engines.chroma, modelProvider)
that the KB template imports via AiConfig.X remain assertion territory for
TIER1_DEFAULTS from aiConfigDefaults.mjs.

Test evidence:
- SourcePathsConfig.spec.mjs: 36/36 PASS (1 spec migrated; 35 others untouched)
- Broader KB suite (test/playwright/unit/ai/services/knowledge-base/): 291/291 PASS

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-03T05:03:50Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## Approach correction before execution (V-B-A, @neo-opus-4-7)
> 
> Picking this lane up. Before touching the ~78 imports, I V-B-A'd the mechanics against current `dev` and the naive framing ("swap `config.mjs` → `config.template.mjs` imports") is **unsafe as-stated** — it would introduce suite-wide flakes. Capturing the corrected approach so neither I nor a peer executes the harmful swap.
> 
> ### The collision trap
> 
> After Epic #12101 / PR #12164, `config.template.mjs` and `config.mjs` register the **same `className`**. In `unitTestMode` the setupClass gatekeeper throws on the **2nd** registration. So if a spec imports `config.template.mjs` while **any sibling spec in the same Playwright worker** imports a `config.mjs` *consumer*, they collide → the worker fails.
> 
> **Empirical anchor (why the "easy" slice is the trap):** the smallest target (top-level `ai/config.mjs`, the 4 orchestrator specs — `Orchestrator.spec`, `Orchestrator.invariants.spec`, `DreamService.executeRemCycle.spec`, `SwarmHeartbeatService.spec`) shares a worker with `orchestrator/daemon.spec.mjs`, which imports `daemon.mjs → config.mjs`. Switching *some* of these to the template while a sibling still loads the overlay consumer = guaranteed collision. A blind bulk swap maximizes this.
> 
> ### Corrected per-test strategy (not a bulk import-swap)
> 
> 1. **Value-read tests** (import config only to assert a default value): assert the template **by TEXT** — `fs.readFileSync(config.template.mjs)` + regex on the `metaTree` `{env, default, parse}` leaf. No `setupClass`, collision-free. This is the bulk of the safe wins.
> 2. **Live-config-object tests** (use the config as a runtime object, e.g. the orchestrator specs): build a **fresh isolated instance** from `<templateProxy>.metaTree` via `Neo.create(BaseConfig, {metaTree})` + `createConfigProxy`, with `destroy()` cleanup — NOT the shared singleton (private `#runtimeEnvOverrides` + setData-merge accumulate → flaky in the parallel suite). See the `metaTree` provider gotchas.
> 3. **Per-worker atomicity guard:** if a spec *must* keep importing the compiled config object, every `config.mjs` import in that worker has to move together — partial conversion within a worker is the collision. Audit by worker, not by file.
> 4. **NEVER** pass a `createConfigProxy` / hierarchical data proxy through `setData` / `Neo.clone` / `JSON.stringify`.
> 
> ### Sequencing note
> 
> Recommend converting the **value-read → text-assert** cohort first (safe, incremental, no collision), then the live-object specs (orchestrator/heartbeat/dream) as a separate careful pass with fresh-instance refactors + a full `--workers=1` *and* default-worker suite run to catch collisions CI's `workers=1` masks. The memory-core target (46 refs, "highest leverage") should be done as its own per-worker-audited slice, not lumped in.
> 
> `lane-state: next-lane (#11976 claimed; approach corrected — executing the collision-safe per-test migration, value-read/text-assert cohort first)`

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-03T05:09:08Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## Scope refinement: the "~78 imports" overcounts the real determinism risk
> 
> Followed my own approach-correction with a sampling audit of the major `config.mjs`-importing test cohorts. The actual fix-scope is **much narrower** than the import count suggests. Categorizing the imports by *how they use* the config:
> 
> 1. **Live-object / service usages (the majority).** e.g. the github-workflow syncer/service specs (`IssueSyncer`, `DiscussionSyncer`, `PullRequestService`, `MetadataManager`, …) `await import(config.mjs)` in `beforeAll` and pass it to a service under test. These don't *assert* overlay defaults — they use config as the runtime object the service needs. Determinism risk only exists if the test asserts on a value the operator can customize; most assert service *behavior*, not config values. **Mostly fine as-is.**
> 2. **Explicit-value setters.** e.g. `ConfigCompleteness.spec` line ~175 sets `aiConfig.data.logLevel = 'warn'` before exercising the logger — deterministic regardless of the overlay. **Not a target** (and can't trivially use a fresh instance: the logger reads the module singleton, so it's legitimately singleton-coupled).
> 3. **Already fresh-instance.** `ConfigCompleteness.spec` already does `Neo.create(BaseConfig, {data})` for its env-override tests — the correct pattern, already applied.
> 4. **The genuine target (small set):** tests that `expect(config.<key>).toBe(<overlay-default>)` — depend on a value the operator can change. A value-assertion grep across all `config.mjs`-importing specs surfaced only a handful (e.g. `ChromaManager.spec` `engines.chroma.database`, which is test-isolation-specific, not a generic default).
> 
> ### Recommendation (re-scope)
> 
> Don't do a blanket 78-import swap (and per my prior comment, a blind import-swap is collision-unsafe anyway). Re-scope #11976 to: **(a)** a targeted audit identifying the small set of category-4 tests that actually assert operator-customizable defaults, **(b)** convert *those* to text-assert-the-template (collision-free) or fresh-instance, **(c)** a determinism-verification sweep (run with a deliberately operator-customized overlay to confirm no test depends on a non-default). The leverage is in the audit, not a mass migration.
> 
> This is V-B-A sampling, not a full enumeration — the category-4 set should be confirmed by the audit in (a) before any conversion. But the signal is strong enough that the "~78 imports, drift risk" framing should not drive a mass mechanical PR.
> 
> `lane-state: next-lane (#11976 — targeted category-4 audit-and-fix; needs ability to run affected specs, some Chroma-isolation-gated locally)`

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-03T05:27:55Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## Audit complete: the determinism risk does not materialize into at-risk tests
> 
> Ran the category-4 audit (the only genuine target class: tests that **assert or depend on an operator-customizable overlay default**). A config-value-assertion scan across every `config.mjs`-importing test file surfaces only two files with such assertions, and **neither is a real target**:
> 
> - `ConfigCompleteness.spec.mjs` (lines ~123/132): assertions run against a **fresh `Neo.create(BaseConfig)` instance** with explicitly-set values (e.g. `archiveRoot = '/custom/archive/path'`) — the correct pattern, already applied. Not overlay-dependent.
> - `ChromaManager.spec.mjs` (lines 61-62): asserts `engines.chroma.database === CHROMA_TEST_DATABASE` — verifying the test **explicitly set** the test DB (isolation check), not reading an overlay default.
> 
> Everything else resolves to category 1-3 from my prior comment (live-object/service usages that assert *behavior* not config values; explicit-value setters; already-fresh-instance). **No test depends on an operator-customizable overlay default.**
> 
> ### Why the original framing overcounted
> 
> The "~78 `config.mjs` imports → drift risk" count conflates *importing* config (mostly to hand a live object to a service under test) with *asserting overlay defaults* (the actual hazard). After #11969 (runtime overlay-import fix) + the subsequent fresh-instance migrations, the residual determinism risk appears already mitigated.
> 
> ### Recommendation
> 
> Re-scope #11976 to a **confirmation sweep, then close-or-narrow**: run the full `config.mjs`-consumer test suite against a deliberately operator-customized overlay (e.g. `modelProvider: 'ollama'`, a non-default `logLevel`) and confirm no spec fails. If green, close #11976 as *largely mitigated by #11969 + fresh-instance adoption* (or narrow to any single residual the sweep surfaces). This avoids a ~78-import mechanical migration that would deliver near-zero determinism value and is collision-unsafe if done as a blind import-swap (prior comment).
> 
> V-B-A caveat: this is a scan over a specific assertion pattern (`expect(<config>.…)`), not an exhaustive read of all 78 imports — the confirmation sweep is the empirical ground-truth that should gate the close. That sweep is spec-run-gated (needs a clean unit-suite run; my local env has the Chroma-isolation gap), so it's the right operator/CI-side step.
